perturbation.original_text: Confidential information that is disclosed orally will be confirmed in writing by the disclosing party within thirty (30) days after such disclosure.
perturbation.changed_text: Confidential information that is disclosed orally will be confirmed in writing by the disclosing party within ninety (90) days after such disclosure.
perturbation.explanation: Modifying the confirmation period for orally disclosed confidential information to 90 days could be considered unreasonable and therefore unenforceable, especially if it prejudices one party's ability to protect their trade secrets effectively. Minnesota's Uniform Trade Secrets Act requires reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy, and a 90-day delay might be seen as a failure to take such measures.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Minnesota Uniform Trade Secrets Act - Section 325C.01 subd. 2
perturbation.law_explanation: Minnesota Statutes, section 325C.01, subd. 2, defines misappropriation partly based on failing to maintain secrecy. A 90-day delay in confirming oral disclosures may not meet the standard of reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy, potentially jeopardizing trade secret protection under Minnesota law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Sec. 325C.01 MN Statutes Skip to main content Skip to office menu Skip to footer Minnesota Legislature Advanced Search Search Legislature Search Office of the Revisor of Statutes Office of the Revisor of Statutes 2024 Minnesota Statutes TRADE REGULATIONS, CONSUMER PROTECTION Chapter 325C Section 325C.01 325C.02 2024 Minnesota Statutes Authenticate PDF Resources Search Minnesota Statutes About Minnesota Statutes 2024 Statutes New, Amended or Repealed 2024 Table of Chapters 2024 Statutes Topics (Index) Chapter 325C Table of Sections Full Chapter Text Version List Section 325C.01 Version List 325C.01 DEFINITIONS. § Subdivision 1. Scope. As used in sections 325C.01 to 325C.07 , the terms defined in these sections have the meanings given them, unless the context requires otherwise. § Subd. 2. Improper means. "Improper means" includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. § Subd. 3. Misappropriation. "Misappropriation" means: (i) acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (ii) disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who (A) used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (B) at the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that the discloser's or user's knowledge of the trade secret was (I) derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (II) acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (III) derived from or through a person who owed a duty to the person seeking relief to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (C) before a material change of the discloser's or user's position, knew or had reason to know that it was a trade secret and that knowledge of it had been acquired by accident or mistake. § Subd. 4. Person.
perturbation.justification: The modification extends the confirmation period for orally disclosed confidential information from 30 to 90 days. The Perturbation and Law Explanations suggest that this could be problematic under Minnesota Statutes, section 325C.01, subd. 2, because it may not constitute 'reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy,' potentially jeopardizing trade secret protection. Scraped Law Snippet 1 defines 'misappropriation' partly based on failing to maintain secrecy. A 90-day delay could be interpreted as a failure to take reasonable measures to protect trade secrets, therefore contradicting the obligation to maintain secrecy as required to avoid misappropriation under Minnesota law.

perturbation.original_text: The "Confidential Period" shall mean two (2) years from the date of receipt of the confidential information or until such time as the information no longer qualifies as confidential information pursuant to paragraph number 5 below.
perturbation.changed_text: The "Confidential Period" shall mean six (6) months from the date of receipt of the confidential information or until such time as the information no longer qualifies as confidential information pursuant to paragraph number 5 below.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the confidential period to six months may not be sufficient to protect certain types of trade secrets, particularly those with a longer commercial lifespan. This could conflict with the 'reasonable efforts' standard required by the Minnesota Uniform Trade Secrets Act (MUTSA).
perturbation.contradicted_law: Minnesota Uniform Trade Secrets Act - Section 325C.01 subd. 2
perturbation.law_explanation: Minnesota Statute 325C.01, subd. 2 requires reasonable efforts to maintain the secrecy of a trade secret. A six-month confidentiality period may be deemed insufficient for certain trade secrets, potentially failing the 'reasonable efforts' standard and risking the loss of trade secret protection under Minnesota law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Sec. 325C.01 MN Statutes Skip to main content Skip to office menu Skip to footer Minnesota Legislature Advanced Search Search Legislature Search Office of the Revisor of Statutes Office of the Revisor of Statutes 2024 Minnesota Statutes TRADE REGULATIONS, CONSUMER PROTECTION Chapter 325C Section 325C.01 325C.02 2024 Minnesota Statutes Authenticate PDF Resources Search Minnesota Statutes About Minnesota Statutes 2024 Statutes New, Amended or Repealed 2024 Table of Chapters 2024 Statutes Topics (Index) Chapter 325C Table of Sections Full Chapter Text Version List Section 325C.01 Version List 325C.01 DEFINITIONS. § Subdivision 1. Scope. As used in sections 325C.01 to 325C.07 , the terms defined in these sections have the meanings given them, unless the context requires otherwise. § Subd. 2. Improper means. "Improper means" includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. § Subd. 3. Misappropriation. "Misappropriation" means: (i) acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (ii) disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who (A) used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (B) at the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that the discloser's or user's knowledge of the trade secret was (I) derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (II) acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (III) derived from or through a person who owed a duty to the person seeking relief to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (C) before a material change of the discloser's or user's position, knew or had reason to know that it was a trade secret and that knowledge of it had been acquired by accident or mistake. § Subd. 4. Person.
perturbation.justification: The modified text reduces the confidentiality period from two years to six months. According to the Law Explanation, Minnesota Statute 325C.01, subd. 2 requires reasonable efforts to maintain the secrecy of a trade secret. The Perturbation Explanation notes that a six-month confidentiality period may be deemed insufficient for certain trade secrets, potentially failing the 'reasonable efforts' standard. Scraped Law Snippet 1 defines 'Improper means' which includes breach of a duty to maintain secrecy and 'Misappropriation' which deals with the acquisition or disclosure of a trade secret by improper means. Thus, reducing the confidentiality period could be seen as failing to make reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy, especially if six months is deemed an insufficient amount of time given the nature of the confidential information.

perturbation.original_text: Further, the receiving party agrees to return to the disclosing party, upon request, any information disclosed in any tangible form, all copies thereof, containing any of the confidential information referred to in paragraph number 1 above.
perturbation.changed_text: Further, the receiving party agrees to return to the disclosing party, only if possible, any information disclosed in any tangible form, all copies thereof, containing any of the confidential information referred to in paragraph number 1 above.
perturbation.explanation: Adding 'only if possible' creates ambiguity and potentially weakens the obligation to return confidential information. This could be seen as a violation of the duty to protect trade secrets, particularly under Minnesota law, which requires reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Minnesota Uniform Trade Secrets Act - Section 325C.01 subd. 2
perturbation.law_explanation: By qualifying the return of confidential information with 'only if possible,' the modified text weakens the receiving party's obligation to protect trade secrets. Minnesota Statute 325C.01, subd. 2 defines misappropriation partly on failing to maintain secrecy, and this change could be interpreted as a failure to take reasonable efforts to do so, potentially leading to loss of trade secret protection.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Sec. 325C.01 MN Statutes Skip to main content Skip to office menu Skip to footer Minnesota Legislature Advanced Search Search Legislature Search Office of the Revisor of Statutes Office of the Revisor of Statutes 2024 Minnesota Statutes TRADE REGULATIONS, CONSUMER PROTECTION Chapter 325C Section 325C.01 325C.02 2024 Minnesota Statutes Authenticate PDF Resources Search Minnesota Statutes About Minnesota Statutes 2024 Statutes New, Amended or Repealed 2024 Table of Chapters 2024 Statutes Topics (Index) Chapter 325C Table of Sections Full Chapter Text Version List Section 325C.01 Version List 325C.01 DEFINITIONS. § Subdivision 1. Scope. As used in sections 325C.01 to 325C.07 , the terms defined in these sections have the meanings given them, unless the context requires otherwise. § Subd. 2. Improper means. "Improper means" includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. § Subd. 3. Misappropriation. "Misappropriation" means: (i) acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (ii) disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who (A) used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (B) at the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that the discloser's or user's knowledge of the trade secret was (I) derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (II) acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (III) derived from or through a person who owed a duty to the person seeking relief to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (C) before a material change of the discloser's or user's position, knew or had reason to know that it was a trade secret and that knowledge of it had been acquired by accident or mistake. § Subd. 4. Person.
perturbation.justification: The added phrase 'only if possible' weakens the receiving party's obligation to return confidential information, creating a contradiction. Minnesota Statute 325C.01, subd. 2, included in Scraped Law Snippet 1, defines misappropriation as a 'breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy'. By making the return of confidential information conditional, the modified text potentially fails to meet the standard of reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy, which could be interpreted as misappropriation. This directly contradicts the legal duty to protect trade secrets under Minnesota law.

perturbation.original_text: Each party hereby affirms that it is not prohibited by the Office of Export Administration for the U.S. Department of Commerce from receiving technical information, know-how, data or other information and each party agrees not to export such information, or products incorporating it, to any prohibited country.
perturbation.changed_text: Each party hereby affirms that it is not prohibited by the Office of Export Administration for the U.S. Department of Commerce from receiving technical information, know-how, data or other information; each party is allowed to export such information, or products incorporating it, to any country.
perturbation.explanation: The original clause complies with export control regulations. The modified clause explicitly allows exporting technical information to *any* country, which directly contradicts U.S. export control laws and regulations administered by the Bureau of Industry and Security (BIS) under the Export Administration Regulations (EAR).
perturbation.law_explanation: 15 CFR § 736.2 outlines the General Prohibitions under the Export Administration Regulations (EAR). The modified text, which allows exporting technical information to any country, directly violates these prohibitions, as exports to certain countries are restricted or require licenses for national security or foreign policy reasons.

We have made every effort to ensure the material presented in these tools are accurate, but if you are relying on them for legal research you should consult the official editions of those sources to confirm your findings. Nothing in these tools binds BIS or creates any rights, benefits, or defenses, substantive or procedural, that are enforceable by any party in any manner. If you find any errors, please email us.

perturbation.justification: The modified text explicitly allows exporting technical information to any country, which directly contradicts U.S. export control laws. The original text affirmed compliance with export restrictions, while the changed text negates this compliance, creating a direct conflict. Snippet 2 references 15 CFR 736.2, which outlines general prohibitions under the Export Administration Regulations (EAR). Allowing exports to 'any country' violates these prohibitions, as certain countries are restricted or require licenses, making the change a clear contradiction.
